control_description: The entity implements logical access security measures to protect against threats from sources outside its system boundaries.

evidence_files.description: Nimbus Analytics system boundary and service mesh security policy requiring mTLS for all production service-to-service communication
Service mesh TLS audit report for 15 production services showing sidecar injection status, PeerAuthentication mode, and mTLS compliance

prompt: You are a SOC 2 auditor evaluating Nimbus Analytics' system boundary controls for the Q4 2025 observation period. Review the boundary security policy and the TLS mesh audit report. Cross-reference the policy's mTLS requirements against the actual service configurations to identify any gaps where services do not meet the stated policy requirements.

findings.title: Schema registry service has PeerAuthentication set to PERMISSIVE instead of STRICT
findings.description: The boundary security policy Section 3.1 requires all production services to use mTLS with PeerAuthentication set to STRICT mode with 'no plaintext fallback.' The TLS mesh audit shows the schema-registry service in the data-ingestion namespace has PeerAuthentication set to PERMISSIVE, which accepts both mTLS and plaintext connections. The audit notes this is due to a 'compatibility issue with legacy Kafka Connect plugin.' Despite having a sidecar injected and a valid Vault certificate, the PERMISSIVE mode means any service — or attacker — can communicate with the schema registry without presenting a client certificate. No formal exception is documented per the Section 3.3 exception process.
findings.expected_reasoning: The auditor should cross-reference the policy requirement (Section 3.1: STRICT mode, no plaintext fallback) with the audit data (schema-registry: PERMISSIVE). PERMISSIVE mode is a direct violation of the policy. The service handles schema definitions for the data ingestion pipeline. Without mTLS enforcement, unauthorized services could query or modify schemas. The compatibility issue with the Kafka Connect plugin is not documented as a formal exception per Section 3.3.

findings.title: Metrics exporter has no Istio sidecar and uses self-signed certificate without mutual authentication
findings.description: The metrics-exporter service in the internal-tools namespace has no Istio sidecar injected and uses a self-signed TLS certificate generated by its Helm chart instead of a Vault-issued certificate. The service has no mutual authentication — it uses server-side TLS only (TLS 1.2) without client certificate validation. The boundary security policy Section 3.1 requires 'every production service must have an Istio sidecar proxy injected' and all certificates must be issued by the internal PKI (HashiCorp Vault). The metrics exporter handles Prometheus metrics export from all namespaces, meaning it has network visibility across the entire production cluster. No formal exception exists per Section 3.3.
findings.expected_reasoning: The auditor should identify that metrics-exporter violates multiple policy requirements: no sidecar (Section 3.1), self-signed certificate instead of Vault-issued (Section 3.2), no mutual authentication, and TLS 1.2 instead of 1.3. The service has broad network access for metrics collection, making it a high-value target. The legacy Helm chart explanation does not constitute a formal exception.

findings.title: Two non-compliant services operate without formal exceptions as required by policy
findings.description: Policy Section 3.3 states that any service that cannot support mTLS 'must obtain a formal exception via the GRC exception process' with business justification, compensating controls, a time-bound remediation plan, and CISO approval. Both the schema-registry (PERMISSIVE mode) and metrics-exporter (no sidecar) are marked NON-COMPLIANT in the audit but have no documented exceptions. The audit notes cite operational reasons (Kafka Connect compatibility, legacy Helm chart) but these are informal explanations, not approved exceptions. The absence of formal exceptions means these gaps have not been risk-assessed, compensating controls have not been documented, and no remediation timeline exists.
findings.expected_reasoning: Beyond the individual technical gaps, the auditor should flag that the exception process itself is not being followed. Two services are non-compliant without formal exceptions, suggesting the exception process may not be well understood by service teams or not enforced by security.
